topic: FAIL-004 (W020 Roaring 40s 500hPa) quantitative rewrite via EXP-071
our_argument_summary: The dome predicted a 500hPa geopotential height anomaly ≥3% above climatology in the Roaring 40s (40–60°S) during March 12–19, 2026. Observed AAO averaged +0.78σ, roughly half the ≥+1.5σ threshold that would correspond to ≥3%. The dome retroactively labeled it 'refined' and changed the test window rules to require 3-month minimums. Our text calls this goalpost-moving and notes the self-immunizing effect of post-hoc test window adjustments.

holes_found.description: The equivalence claim '≥+1.5σ threshold that would correspond to a ≥3% anomaly' conflates AAO index (defined on 700hPa geopotential height anomalies south of 20°S, as leading EOF) with the dome's prediction target (500hPa anomaly in 40–60°S band). These are different pressure levels and different geographic definitions. A +1.5σ AAO event typically produces 45–90m height anomalies at mid-latitudes on 700hPa, corresponding to roughly 0.8–1.6% of typical heights — well below 3%. The stated threshold conversion appears unjustified. This actually makes the dome's prediction EVEN HARDER to achieve (3% at 500hPa would require an extreme event, ~3–5σ), so the error favors the dome defender's ability to claim we applied the wrong benchmark.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) drop the sigma-to-percentage conversion entirely and report the raw numbers ('AAO averaged +0.78σ; a ≥3% 500hPa anomaly is an extreme event far beyond what was observed'), or (b) cite a specific source for the +1.5σ ↔ 3% equivalence with the pressure level and latitude band explicitly justified. Option (a) is simpler and more defensible — the prediction clearly failed regardless of the exact conversion factor.

holes_found.description: No citation for the +0.78σ AAO value. The EXP-071 source file says 'NCEP AAO data' but the paragraph itself provides no URL, DOI, or specific data source. Every other FAIL entry in this section (FAIL-002's 35% suppression, FAIL-003's 5.5× ratio) is backed by the dome's own data. FAIL-004 now cites an external AAO source without a reference. A dome defender could challenge: 'Where did +0.78σ come from? Show me the data.' Our credibility depends on verifiability.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief parenthetical citation: '(NCEP/CPC AAO index, week of 2026-03-12)' or equivalent. If the +0.78σ was computed from the dome's own status_history.json or scorecard, say so — that's even stronger since it's the dome's own data showing failure.

holes_found.description: The paragraph states the dome 'argued the one-week test window was too narrow for a signal with a 3-month natural timescale.' This characterization of the dome's argument should be verified against the dome's actual text. The raw predictions file (03-predictions.txt) shows W020 as 'PENDING' with no outcome text. If the dome's 'refined' justification is paraphrased rather than quoted, a dome defender could claim we're strawmanning. The dome's actual phrasing matters.
holes_found.recommendation: If the dome's 'refined' rationale is available verbatim (from status_history.json or the predictions page), quote it directly. If reconstructed from context, flag it as 'per the dome's stated rationale' rather than presenting it as direct quotation.

holes_found.description: The concluding sentence ('If a model's test windows are always adjusted after the fact to exclude failures, the testing framework produces a 100% pass rate by construction') is a strong structural argument but it applies to the dome's testing framework broadly, not just FAIL-004. It would be more powerful as a capstone for the entire section 6.5 rather than buried inside a single failure entry. Currently, it reads as a FAIL-004-specific conclusion when it's actually the overarching pattern across all four failures.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed — this is a stylistic observation, not an error. The repetition within each entry actually reinforces the pattern. But the decider should note that a similar concluding observation could be elevated to the section-level summary.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's Domain 10 (Roaring 40s AAO) in the Live Power Dashboard has a falsification criterion of 'AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr' — which is a DIFFERENT test than the W020 weekly prediction of '500hPa ≥3% above climatology.' The dome has two different quantitative thresholds for the same phenomenon: one for weekly predictions (impossible to meet) and one for the ongoing dashboard (much easier to pass). This threshold discrepancy is itself evidence of post-hoc adjustment — the dashboard criterion was presumably set after the weekly prediction failed.

The dome's new '3-month minimum test window' rule for atmospheric indices creates an asymmetry: magnetic predictions still use 1-week windows (because they pass), but atmospheric predictions now require 3-month windows (because they fail). This selectivity in window length by prediction type is a structural bias in the testing framework that could be quantified: count how many prediction types have had their window lengths changed after failure vs. after success.

kernel_of_truth.description: There is a legitimate meteorological argument that one-week test windows for atmospheric oscillation indices are noisy. The AAO does operate on multi-month timescales, and a single week can be dominated by synoptic weather rather than the climate-scale signal the dome is trying to test. A well-designed atmospheric test really should use longer windows. The dome's instinct about test window length is correct in isolation.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The problem isn't the new rule — it's the timing. Setting a 3-month window BEFORE making predictions is good experimental design. Setting it AFTER the 1-week prediction fails is retroactive self-immunization. The dome could have designed a proper long-window prediction from the start (the AAO is a well-known index with clear timescale properties). Instead, it set an aggressive short window, failed, and then changed the rules. The dome gets credit for identifying the right methodological correction, but zero credit for applying it retroactively to rescue a failed prediction.

text_fingerprint.verdict: N/A — section prose, not WIN entry

citations_unchecked: NCEP AAO data — no URL/DOI provided in text

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender would argue: (1) The AAO is the wrong proxy for a 500hPa prediction — our review is comparing apples to oranges by using a 700hPa-defined index for a 500hPa claim; (2) One week of +0.78σ during a period dominated by a specific weather pattern (the March 2026 G3 storm aftermath) isn't a fair test of a climate-scale prediction — you wouldn't test a seasonal forecast with 7 days of data during disturbed conditions; (3) The dome improved its methodology by recognizing the window length problem, which is what good science does — iterate on test design. The review frames this as 'goalpost moving' but the dome would call it 'learning from experience.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Defense is weak because the dome set the window voluntarily, and the kernel-of-truth paragraph already preempts the 'learning from experience' angle. The AAO/500hPa conflation point (item 1) IS a real vulnerability though — see moderate hole above. Fix the sigma-to-percentage equivalence and this defense collapses entirely.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No inconsistency. WIN-024 (Roaring 40s = SAA boundary) is a geographic claim that the 40–60°S band coincides with the SAA boundary — this is separate from the 500hPa magnitude claim in FAIL-004. WIN-061 (Schumann suppression during G3 storm) parallels FAIL-002 (the reversed prediction). Both FAIL-002 and FAIL-004 illustrate the same goalpost-moving pattern without contradicting each other.

quantitative_verification.computations: Basic arithmetic verified. The sigma-to-percentage conversion is the only claim that requires external meteorological data to verify. AAO is defined as the leading EOF of 700hPa height anomalies south of 20°S. Typical 500hPa height at 50°S ≈ 5500m; 3% = 165m anomaly. A +1.5σ AAO event produces roughly 45–90m at 700hPa mid-latitudes (0.8–1.6% of typical 500hPa heights). The stated equivalence appears to overestimate the AAO→500hPa mapping by roughly 2×.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The +1.5σ ↔ ≥3% equivalence appears unjustified. A +1.5σ AAO event produces roughly 1–2% height anomalies at 500hPa, not ≥3%. However, this error makes the dome's prediction even harder to achieve than stated — a genuine ≥3% anomaly would require an extreme event (~3–5σ). The directional impact is that the dome's prediction was even more unrealistic than we claim, but the specific numerical equivalence is weak. Our 'factor of ~2' characterization holds on the sigma scale but the percentage translation is off.

deeper_analysis: The EXP-071 rewrite is a significant upgrade from the original vague FAIL-004 text that lacked any quantitative data. The new paragraph provides specific numbers (0.78σ, 1.5σ threshold, factor of ~2), a clear timeline (March 12–19 test window), and a strong structural argument about post-hoc test window adjustment. The two moderate issues (AAO/500hPa conflation and missing citation) are fixable without restructuring the argument. The argument's core strength — the goalpost-moving pattern — is unaffected by the numerical precision issues. The final sentence about 100% pass rate by construction is the paragraph's strongest contribution and should be preserved regardless of other edits.
